entry.title: Re: Onboarding - Rehire with old Employment Error
entry.content:
Hi @Ludmi - if you haven't already, please raise a support ticket. Even though an application error may be happening due to bad data or config, ideally the system should still return a more descriptive message rather than a generic application error. I believe it is justified to ask for a better error message here; this will make life easier for all customers as well as for Support/Engineering as it prevents future tickets of the same sort.

Best regards,
Janina

entry.title: Re: SQL Error in HANA Studio Seems to be Cached
entry.content:
Hi,

This error you describe “could not split statements; sql console content is too large" is a very typical occurrence when executing a "HANA_Configuration_MiniChecks_2.00.085+.txt" report for example.

However the statement will typically keep running without fail if you check the green progress bar on the bottom right hand side in HANA Studio. So it is usually more of a warning and an actual exception failure.

I have encountered this many times during Mini check execution and the statement never actually failed because of this error, so I would suggest monitoring. If the statement does fail it will likely show an updated error reason in the same "result" pane where the original error appears.

Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: How SAP Signavio, LeanIX, CALM & SuccessFactors Form a Unified HR IT Architecture
entry.content:
Why This Architecture Matters for HR IT

HR IT landscapes have never been more complex. The average enterprise runs 10–20 HR-related applications, spanning core HR, talent, learning, compensation, and workforce analytics. Yet most architecture reviews reveal the same pattern: process documentation lives in Visio, capabilities are tracked in a spreadsheet, system landscapes are managed informally, and SuccessFactors modules are configured without reference to any of the above.

The result? Change requests take longer than they should. Duplicate applications persist because no-one has a complete picture. Process owners don't know which applications support their process — and application owners don't know which business processes they enable.

💡

Architecture Principle: Every HR application should be traceable to a business capability. Every capability should be traceable to a business process. Every process should have a named owner from your SuccessFactors org structure.

 

The Four-Layer Architecture at a Glance

Think of the architecture as four layers stacked from business intent down to technical delivery — with a critical feedback loop running back up from people data to process ownership.

 

Figure 1: Process-to-People — Integrated HR IT Architecture Overview

 

Layer

Tool

What It Does in HR IT

Key Output

L1 — Business Process

SAP Signavio

Models end-to-end HR processes (Recruit-to-Hire, Hire-to-Retire, Learn-to-Grow, Pay-to-Reward) with BPMN notation and assigns process owners

Process catalogue with ownership & KPIs

L2 — HR Capabilities

LeanIX

Maps HR business capabilities to applications, identifies redundancies, scores maturity, and links to the process layer above

HR capability heat map + app portfolio

L3 — System Landscape

SAP CALM

Tracks the technical landscape of all HR systems (Dev/QA/Prod), manages transports and change pipelines, and monitors integration health via BTP

Live HR system topology + change log

L4 — HR Applications

SAP SuccessFactors

Delivers core HR, talent, learning, and compensation capabilities. Acts as the system of record for org structure and people data — which feeds back to all layers above

HR master data + module configurations

 

Layer 1: SAP Signavio — Defining the HR Process Backbone

SAP Signavio is where the HR architecture begins. Before you can map capabilities or configure SuccessFactors, you need to agree on what your HR processes actually are — and who owns them. In Signavio, HR IT teams model four core end-to-end HR value chains using BPMN 2.0:

Recruit-to-Hire

  • Job requisition and approval workflow
  • Candidate sourcing, screening, and interview management
  • Offer management and pre-boarding
  • Onboarding tasks and new hire orientation

Hire-to-Retire

  • Employee master data management in SF Employee Central
  • Performance review and goal-setting cycles
  • Absence, leave, and time management
  • Offboarding, exit interviews, and alumni management

Learn-to-Grow

  • Learning needs assessment and curriculum planning
  • Course catalogue management and delivery (ILT + e-learning)
  • Skills and competency tracking
  • Career development and succession planning

Pay-to-Reward

  • Payroll calculation, validation, and posting
  • Compensation planning cycles and merit reviews
  • Benefits enrolment and administration
  • Total reward statements

✅

Best Practice: Tag each Signavio process with the SuccessFactors module that supports it. This single annotation creates the first link in your process-to-application traceability chain — and saves hours of analysis when you need to assess the impact of a SuccessFactors upgrade.

 

Layer 2: LeanIX — The HR Capability Map

Once processes are documented in Signavio, LeanIX translates them into business capabilities — the stable building blocks of what your HR function needs to be able to do, regardless of which application delivers it. This separation is deliberate: capabilities change slowly, applications change frequently.

 

HR Capability

Primary App

Secondary App

Maturity

Talent Acquisition

SF Recruiting

DocuSign

L3

Core HR & Employee Data

SF Employee Central

—

L4

Performance & Goals

SF Performance

MS Viva Goals

L3

Learning & Development

SF Learning

Degreed

L2

Compensation & Benefits

SF Compensation

Benefitfocus

L3

HR Analytics & Reporting

SF People Analytics

SAP Analytics Cloud

L2

 

The maturity score (L1–L5) is a LeanIX-standard metric that surfaces where your HR capabilities are underpowered — enabling a fact-based conversation with the business about investment priorities.

Layer 3: SAP CALM — Managing the HR System Landscape

With capabilities mapped in LeanIX, SAP CALM gives you the technical view: which systems are deployed, how they are connected, and how changes move through the landscape. For SuccessFactors customers, CALM provides three critical functions:

Integration monitoring — CALM tracks all BTP/Cloud Integration (CPI) iFlows that connect SuccessFactors to third-party systems (payroll, LMS, ERP). When an iFlow breaks, CALM surfaces it before the business notices.

Change & transport management — All SuccessFactors configuration changes (picklist updates, workflow changes, role-based permissions) are tracked in CALM as change documents, giving you a full audit trail and enabling coordinated release cycles across Dev, QA, and Production.

Landscape documentation — CALM automatically discovers and maps your HR system topology, keeping LeanIX's application portfolio in sync without manual updates.

⚠️

Common Pitfall: Many SuccessFactors customers manage transports and integration monitoring in separate spreadsheets and email threads. CALM eliminates this by providing a single, audit-ready change log. If you are preparing for an SAP audit or a major release, this alone justifies the investment.

 

Layer 4: SAP SuccessFactors — Where the Architecture Meets the Employee

SAP SuccessFactors is both the delivery layer and the data source for the entire architecture. Its modules deliver the capabilities defined in LeanIX, execute the processes modelled in Signavio, and run in the landscape managed by CALM. But critically, SuccessFactors also feeds back upwards:

The org structure in Employee Central defines the process owners referenced in Signavio.

The job architecture (job families, grades, functions) drives the capability ownership in LeanIX.

The role-based permissions in SuccessFactors align with the access model defined at the architecture level.

This bidirectional relationship is the key insight of the Process-to-People model — SuccessFactors is not just an application at the bottom of the stack. It is the authoritative source of organisational truth that gives meaning to everything above it.

 

Detailed HR IT Architecture Diagram

The diagram below shows the full capability and process detail across all four layers, including the specific SuccessFactors modules mapped to each HR capability.

 

Figure 2: HR IT Architecture — Capability & Process Detail with SuccessFactors Module Mapping

 

Key Takeaways for HR IT Leaders

  1. Start with Signavio. Document your four core HR processes first. Without process clarity, capability mapping and system configuration have no anchor.
  2. Use LeanIX to surface redundancy. Most organisations discover 2–4 duplicate HR capabilities once the portfolio is mapped. Consolidation frees budget for capability gaps.
  3. Connect CALM to your SuccessFactors release cycle. Every half-yearly SAP release becomes a managed event rather than an emergency when CALM is tracking your landscape.
  4. Treat SuccessFactors EC as your org master. Sync process ownership in Signavio and application ownership in LeanIX to your EC org hierarchy — not to a separate spreadsheet.
  5. Close the loop. The feedback from SuccessFactors people data back to Signavio and LeanIX is what keeps the architecture live. Without it, your process models and capability maps become stale within months.

 

Getting Started: A Practical Roadmap

You do not need to implement all four tools simultaneously. A pragmatic sequence for HR IT organisations:

Phase 1 (0–3 months): Model your top two HR processes in Signavio. Focus on Hire-to-Retire and Recruit-to-Hire as they typically have the broadest SuccessFactors footprint.

Phase 2 (3–6 months): Stand up a LeanIX HR capability map seeded from your Signavio process output. Map your existing SuccessFactors modules to capabilities and score maturity.

Phase 3 (6–12 months): Onboard CALM for integration monitoring and transport management. Connect the CALM system inventory to LeanIX via the standard integration.

Ongoing: Maintain the SuccessFactors org structure as the authoritative source for process and capability ownership across all tools.

ℹ️

Related Resources: SAP Best Practices content for SuccessFactors is available via the SAP Upgrade Center and can be imported directly into Signavio to accelerate your process modelling in Phase 1. See the SAP Help Portal for the latest activation guide.

entry.title: Requirement for Restriction on Goods Issue Against Rejected Reservations
entry.content:
Dear  Experts,

during our daily operations it was observed that material can still be issued through MIGO even when the related reservation has been rejected by the approving authority.

Further, while generating the reservation list through T-code MB25, the rejected reservations are also appearing in the list of open items, which is creating confusion for the store personnel during material issuance.

Our requirements remain as follows:

  1. The system should restrict goods issue in MIGO for reservations that have been rejected. Only approved reservations should be eligible for material issuance.
  2. Rejected reservations should not appear in the reservation list/report generated through T-code MB25.

Kindly review this matter and provide a suitable solution or configuration at the earliest.

Your prompt response in this matter will be highly appreciated.

entry.title: PORTAL - Understanding the Need for Secure & Responsible AI at SAP
entry.content:
In one of the most crucial sectors – Aviation, safety relies on identifying and mitigating risks through robust, multi-layered systems designed to counter cognitive biases and organizational failures. Modern safety management integrates human factors, data-driven analytics, and rigorous testing to improve reliability and trust. Aircrafts need to go through thorough inspection and testing to prove their airworthiness before they are permitted to operate. Similarly, SAP’s Global AI Ethics Policy emphasizes principles such as fairness, privacy, robustness, transparency, and security. When SAP develops its own AI capabilities, these principles guide every phase—from dataset curation to deployment, with model benchmarking and evaluation being a critical part to ensure AI safety and security (see the keep_the_blue_side_up blogpost series).

One area of growing importance is SAP’s work with in-house foundation models. While many assume SAP only integrates external LLMs, the reality is different: SAP also builds its own foundational models tailored to business data, enterprise processes, and multi-modal use cases. One of these is the research work PORTAL.

For foundation models, it is important to ensure they are resilient and secure. Our recent evaluation of the research work PORTAL focuses exactly on this: understanding how PORTAL behaves when subjected to adversarial stress tests, privacy attacks, distribution shifts, and robustness checks. 
These findings are summarized here in a transparent and accessible way, based on the detailed technical analysis conducted internally.

Why Security and Robustness Testing Is Critical for Foundation Models

Foundation models learn from vast quantities of structured (tabular) and sometimes unstructured data. PORTAL is trained on substantial amount of tabular data. This gives foundation models impressive generalization capabilities but also exposes them to potential vulnerabilities.

To ensure that PORTAL meets SAP’s enterprise requirements, we evaluated it against a curated subset of the OWASP Top 10 for Machine Learning, focusing on attacks that are relevant to proprietary foundation models

 What Is PORTAL? 

PORTAL is an SAP research work for table-native foundation models. It:

  • learns from diverse, business-specific tabular datasets
  • supports multiple downstream tasks
  • can be fine-tuned for various enterprise scenarios
  • follows SAP’s policy-aligned AI safeguards by design

The following section presents the experiments conducted on PORTAL to assess the applicability of relevant OWASP threats within enterprise-focused environments.

Experiment : Sequential Fine-Tuning — Does PORTAL Retain Knowledge Safely?

We began by testing whether PORTAL can retain information across sequential training without leaking old data or overfitting to new data. We performed tests using the Breast Cancer dataset, which was available readily & had nearly equal distribution across various features & labels.

Case 1 — Extreme Distribution Shift (Label Flip)

Flipping class labels produced a dramatic drop in accuracy:

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After fine-tuning on flipped data: 8.07%

Interpretation: 
The model exhibited catastrophic forgetting, in that performance on the earlier fine-tuned task degraded substantially after adaptation to a strongly shifted distribution (label flip). This suggests that the model did not retain information from the previous fine-tuned dataset once trained on the new task, indicating overwriting of task-specific patterns during sequential fine-tuning. We emphasize that this observation is limited to the fine-tuning stage and does not provide evidence regarding memorization during pre-training.

Case 2 — Mild Feature Perturbations

We applied small additive and multiplicative Gaussian noise to simulate mild distribution shifts and fine-tuned the model on the perturbed data.

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After perturbation fine-tuning: 41.05%

Interpretation: 
PORTAL adapts better to controlled shifts. 
This demonstrates better generalization while still maintaining partial retention — the desirable behavior for enterprise adaptation, where gradual data drift is common. 

.      

Figure 1: Left: Case 1; Right: Case 2

Experiment : Membership Inference Attacks — Does PORTAL Leak Training Data? 
To evaluate privacy resilience, we analyzed whether PORTAL assigns systematically higher confidence scores to samples seen during recent fine-tuning compared to held-out samples. This experiment focuses exclusively on detecting potential membership signals in fine-tuning data and does not target memorization from pre-training.

Two datasets were used during fine-tuning:

  • 569-sample Breast Cancer dataset
  • 1000-sample augmented dataset (over-sampling)

For both settings, we compared the distributions of true-class confidence scores for member (train) and non-member (test/held out data) samples.

Findings: 

  • Smaller dataset → Larger confidence gap → Potentially higher privacy risk: In the smaller dataset setting, a mild shift in confidence distributions is observed, consistent with limited overfitting.
  • Larger dataset → Reduced confidence gap → Better privacy protection: In the augmented dataset setting, member and non-member confidence distributions exhibit strong overlap, indicating the absence of clear confidence-based separability.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Importantly, in neither setting does a reliable confidence threshold emerge that would enable effective membership inference. The increased overlap in the larger dataset further reduces residual leakage signals.

These results suggest that PORTAL exhibits low practical vulnerability to confidence-based membership inference attacks, and that increased data diversity and scale further enhance privacy robustness.  We emphasize that extracting memorized information from pre-training is substantially more challenging and remains outside the scope of this study.

Experiment : Knife-Edge Analysis — Understanding Feature Sensitivity

Knife-edge analysis helps identify features where very small changes can sharply flip the model’s prediction. This reveals whether the model is relying on balanced information or leaning too heavily on a single dominant signal. Two complementary tests were conducted:

  1. Global Sensitivity Test (Feature Shuffling / Permutation)
  2. Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Feature Increment)

Comparative evaluation was performed on: PORTAL, Decision Tree, XGBoost

Case 1 -  Breast Cancer Dataset (Comparative Analysis) 

(The task is binary classification where, 0 = Malignant, 1 = Benign) 

Feature Importance Analysis: Before conducting knife-edge tests, we examined which features each model relied on most.

  • Portal: Mean Radius and Mean Perimeter, more weightage on Mean Radius
  • Decision Tree: Worst Radius has highest feature relevance
  • XGBoost: Worst Perimeter has the highest feature relevance 

The knife-edge experiments were then performed on the highest-ranked feature for each model.

.          . 

      PORTAL                                            Decision Tree                                                XGBoost

Global Sensitivity Test (Permutation): 

We shuffled the dominant feature for each model and measured accuracy change.

Run 1: 

       

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                                      XGBoost

Feature shuffled per model: PORTAL (Mean Radius), Decision Tree (Worst Radius), XGBoost (Worst Perimeter)

Run 

PORTAL Baseline (%) (Training) 

PORTAL Drop (%)(Testing) 

DT Baseline (%)(Training) 

DT Drop (%)(Testing) 

XGB Baseline (%)(Training) 

XGB Drop (%)(Testing) 

Run 1 

90.35 

13.16 

91.23 

7.02 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 2 

87.72 

16.67 

91.23 

5.26 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 3 

92.98 

17.54 

91.23 

3.51 

94.74 

−2.63 

Overall, the findings suggest that PORTAL relies more heavily on a single dominant feature, whereas tree-based models achieve greater robustness through distributed feature utilization.

Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Analysis)

We selected a correctly predicted benign sample row:

  • mean radius = 11.13 (considered for PORTAL)
  • worst radius = 11.68 (considered for Decision Tree)
  • worst perimeter = 74.35 (considered for XGBoost)
  • true label = 1

We gradually increased the dominant feature (per model) from its original value in the selected sample up to the maximum observed value for that feature, while keeping all other features constant.

Observations:

     

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                             XGBoost

Comparative Sensitivity Summary:

Model 

Dominant Feature 

Global Accuracy Drop 

Local Flip Detected 

PORTAL  

Mean Radius 

High (−18.42%) 

Yes (at 16.03) 

Decision Tree 

Worst Radius 

Moderate (−7.02%) 

No 

XGBoost 

Worst Perimeter 

Minimal (+0.88%) 

No 

The results suggest that PORTAL places stronger emphasis on a single dominant scalar feature, whereas tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), despite assigning high importance to certain features, leverage a more distributed set of inputs during prediction.

Case 2 - Diabetes Dataset (PORTAL Corroboration Study)

The pattern repeated in the Diabetes dataset. 
When gradually changing Age, the prediction flipped from Diabetic → Non-diabetic at Age = 21, while adjusting Pregnancies had no impact. Again, one dominant feature drove the prediction.

    

Case 3 - Column Reordering

We also tested whether feature ordering affected predictions. Before reordering, occasional flips occurred during perturbations. After reordering, flips disappeared within the tested range — indicating that even column order influenced inference behaviour. 

    

For tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), column reordering was not applicable. These models bind feature indices during training and enforce strict feature-name alignment at inference time. As a result, predictions remain invariant to column order, and any mismatch in ordering typically raises a validation error rather than altering model output.

Overall Observation

Knife-edge evaluation across datasets shows that PORTAL tends to anchor its decisions around a few dominant scalar features. This can make the model efficient but also makes it more sensitive to noise and small perturbations. These insights help guide improvements in regularization and encourage more distributed feature learning, strengthening the model's robustness for real-world enterprise scenarios.

Experiment : Memorization Testing — Distinguishing True Learning from Learned Shortcuts

This experiment was conducted on the publicly available tutorial dataset containing multiple modality-based categories. We reduced the proportion of “audio” samples in the training set from 40% to 10% while increasing their share in the test set, forcing the model to classify predominantly unseen audio data. This means that, in the final case, the model was asked to classify 90% audio samples it had never seen before. Prior to splitting, explicit duplicate removal was performed between training and test sets to mitigate trivial data leakage.

Unexpected Outcome: Across every scenario, including when the model had seen only a small fraction of a category, the accuracy remained exceptionally high (99–100%). At first glance, this could be mistaken for excellent generalization. However, such results usually indicate the opposite.

Why This Matters

A model that performs perfectly on data it has never been trained on is often relying on:

  • memorized patterns from the initial training samples,
  • dataset artifacts, or
  • shortcut cues that do not represent true category understanding.

Why This Insight Is Important

If a model relies on memorized cues rather than meaningful learning, its performance could degrade significantly when exposed to real-world enterprise data, where such shortcuts may not exist. By identifying memorization tendencies early, we can:

  • Expand dataset diversity
  • Strengthen model robustness
  • Improve generalization across unseen business scenarios

Conclusion:

This evaluation helped us understand how PORTAL behaves under different security, robustness, and privacy conditions. Each experiment revealed meaningful insights - from how the model adapts to distribution shifts, to how it handles sensitive features, to where shortcut learning or memorization may appear. These findings directly support SAP’s goal of strengthening responsible, secure, and reliable foundation models for enterprise use. Analogically ensuring the flight is smooth, safe and reliable. PORTAL is another example of how AI transparency, robustness, and responsible evaluation help “keep the blue side up”.                                              

References:

  1. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.12715
  2. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial/blob/main/Tutorial_Example_Dataset.csv
  3. https://help.sap.com/docs/data-attribute-recommendation
  4. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial
  5. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2409.01380

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Conversion Check list Steps by Steps with Details.
entry.content:
Complete checklist of converting SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA.


Overview of SAP S/4HANA Conversion.

Rise with SAP S/4 Conversion refers to the process of technically and functionally transforming an existing SAP ECC  System into SAP S/4HANA, SAP’s next‑generation ERP platform. This approach is also known as System Conversion.

Why i am creating this Blog?

When I first received the S/4HANA Conversion project, I did not know where to start because I did not have a proper checklist. At that time, it was challenging to understand the complete starting point and sequence of activities for an S/4HANA Conversion.
Then i decided that after completing this S/4HANA Conversion project, I would create a detailed, step‑by‑step checklist covering all phases and activities. The objective of this checklist is to ensure that, in the future, no one faces confusion about where and how to begin an S/4HANA Conversion project.

 

We can do Conversion with two methods.

DMO with System Move and DMOVE2S4.

Key Differences between DMO with System Move and dmove2s4.

DMO with System Move   

1  Downtime Optimization           Limited downtime optimization
2  Data Transfer Method               File-based transfer (slower)
3  SUM Run Location                    Starts on the source system and completes on the target system.
4  Network                                       Intermediate network connectivity
5  Architecture                                Operates largely as a two-part move. The first part runs on the source primary application server (PAS), exporting data, while the final part runs on the target.
6  Use                                               Suitable for cases where the strict network prerequisites of DMOVE2S4 cannot be fulfilled.
.

 

DMOVE2S4 (Newer Method)

1  Downtime Optimization     Supports downtime‑optimized DMO (doDMO)
2  Data Transfer Method         R3load pipe mode (direct memory transfer)
3  SUM Run Location               Triggered from the target system’s Additional Application Server (AAS)
4  Network                                  High‑performance network required (Latency < 20 ms, bandwidth > 400 Mbps)
5  Architecture                            An additional application server is installed in the target environment and connected to the source database. The Software Update Manager is executed from this target AAS.
6  Use                                          DMOVE2S4 is used for fast migration to SAP S/4HANA in cloud environments such as Azure or AWS with minimal system downtime..

 

Step1 .

The S/4HANA conversion journey is shown below.

 

 

Step2 .  

Source System Landscape Details.   (Platform is on Premise)

Operating Systems-----> HP UX B.11.31 Uia640225047834.
Application-----------> EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 SPS level is 10.
SAP NETWEAVER---------->7.5 SPS level is 11.
Database------------------>Oracle 12.1.0.2.0
Kernel----------------->753 Patch Level 201
SAP_BASIS-------------->750 SP is 20.
Unicode---------------->Yes.
Single Stack Only------>ABAP.
Number of CPU is------->8
Physical Memory--------32

 

Step3 .

Target System Landscape Details.  (Platform is RISE with SAP.)

Operating Systems------->Linux SUSE SP5.
Application------------->SAP S/4HANA 2023 SPS level 03.
Database----------------->HANA 2.00.083.00.174064
HANA Client-------------->2.0
SAP_BASIS----------------> Release 758 SP level 03.
SAP_ABA------------------->Release 75I SP level 03.
Kernel-------------------->793 Patch level 300.
Unicode------------------->Yes

 

Step4 .

Migration Server Details.

Default Migration Server SID is LT1. 

Port Number 22, 1128 , 1129 and 4237 need to opened and Telnet Should be completed Successfully.
On Migration Server (LT1). We need to install SAP AS ABAP Stub Installation.

 

Step5 .

These Ports need to open in Migration Server.
Port 22 is used for secure access via PuTTY (SSH).
Ports 1128 (HTTP) and 1129 (HTTPS) are utilized for SUM (Software Update Manager) execution.
Port 4237 is required for SWPM during AS ABAP Stub installation.

 

Step6 .

What is Migration Server?

Migration Server used to move the data from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

The system performs data transfer as part of the SUM DMO migration process.

In Migration Server Default SID is LT1.

 

Step7 .

Below are the team details required during the conversion process.

Infra Team-----Responsible for Build the Hardware and Operating System and File System.
Network Team--Responsible for Port opening and Firewall and DNS Configuration.
Basis Team------Responsible for Technical S/4 Conversion ,  S/4HANA Installation ,Upgradation , Unicode Conversion, System Refresh SID Change , Backup Configuration , PRE and Post Steps.
Security Team-----Responsible for Users, Roles and Authorization and PRE and Post Steps.
Functional Team--Responsible for Functional Conversion PRE and Post Steps.
PS Team-------------Responsible for Fund Management.

 

Step8 .

Below is Architecture of Source  and  Target Server.

 

 

Step9 .

These checks help to determine the mandatory activities required before initiating the SAP S/4HANA system conversion.

System Requirement.

Maintenance Planner.

SI-Check / Readiness Check

Custom-code analysis.

 

Step10 .

Basis Steps.

The system must be running SAP ERP ECC 6.0 with Enhancement Package levels ranging from EHP 0 to EHP 8.
Your system must be an AS ABAP-only system. If your system is as dual-stack system, you have to split it before
doing the conversion.
Verify the current database size.
Source and target Server timings should be the same for both the database and application layers.
Ensure host file entries are updated on both source and target systems, and increase tablespaces and datafiles as required.
Verify that hardware requirements, including CPU, RAM, and file system space, are adequate and properly sized.
Execute a full System backup and create a sandbox environment by copying the latest Production System Data.
Post step in Sandbox system Like BDLS,SGEN, SMLG,SM61,RZ10.
Ensure Password Availability of all standard users.
SAP_COLLECTOR_FOR_PERFMONITOR Job should be running and Latest Data should be Sync and reflective in ST03N.
Update the DB Statistics before starting the to avoid the issue.
Complete TCI Enablement activity on Source Sandbox system.
SAP Backbone Configuration in Sandbox for Download the SAP Notes.
Upgrade SAP Host Agent and SAPOSCOL should be running.
066 and 001 Client Deletion as per SAP note 1749142.
SDBI_CLUSTER_CHECK report needs to be run.
SDBI_POOL_CHECK report needs to be run.
SDBI_CHECK_BCD_NUMBERS report needs to be run.
Check and clear inconsistencies (missing tables & indexes) reported in DB02/DBACOCKPIT.
Ensure that all add‑ons and patch levels are consistent across the entire system landscape.
Make sure there are no invalid temp tables exists in SE14.
Process the pending update records and clear obsolete update records - SM13.
Clear invalid lock entries in SM12.
Process pending queues and clear obsolete queues SMQ1, SMQ2 and transactional RFCs SM58.
Check & Update correct hostname in SMLG, RZ12, STRUSTSSO2,SM59.
"Ensure the settings are correct for installed languages and code pages via / RSCPINST report /
se38 > rscpinst > select both and check."
Verify the current number of background processes and increase them based on hardware capacity and system workload.
Remove any unused profile or old profiles from the profile directory. RZ10
Before you start add the SAPSYSTEM ( and env. variable SAPSYSTEMNAME) parameter to the DEFAULT/INSTANCE profile of your System.
The entry should look like: SAPSYSTEM = <instance number> (if already you can skip this step.)
Run TEMSE Consistency check and make sure that there are no error messages in SP12 transaction.
Check that there are no unconfirmed Support Packages in your SAP system. SPAM
Check and configure Transport Management System - STMS . Do STMS Test in all app servers.
Delete the parameter AUTOSTART = 1, if it is set, in the start profile.
Check & Update the Profile parameters - DIR_TRANS, DIR_PUT & DIR_EPS_ROOT.
Make sure that parameter rdisp/bufrefmode has the following values in the DEFAULT.PFL (sendon,exeauto for multiple apps and sendoff, exeauto for single app).
Make sure that parameter rdisp/bufrefmode is not set in the instance profile.
Run the command ""sapcontrol -nr <instance Number> -function GetProcessList {Stop/Start/Restart}.
The output should show all in green. Else need to be corrected on all app servers.
Change parameter value rdisp/max_wprun_time to your desire value.
Configure the RFC FINBTR@<system>CLNT<client> with DDIC user if only we have the FINBASIS Component in ECC.
Delete Expired Application Logs in System.
As per this Note 3589635 latency of under 20 ms and a bandwidth exceeding 400 Mbps between Source and Target Servers. Please read this Note.
Telnet should be working from Source System to LT1 Server.
VPN Tunnel should be open from SAP to Customer Firewall system.
Time Zone Should be same from LT1 to Source system.
Creating a backup of T512W table in Every Client of system.
Check whether any third‑party components are present in the system. If third‑party components exist, analyze the system with respect to those components.
Slicense should be applied in system.
Uninstall SAP Fiori Apps and Prepare the Conversion of Authorizations & for SAP Fiori UX Enablement.
Check the current database version, kernel version, and patch level. If they do not meet the required level, upgrade them according to the target system requirements.
Check the APP and DB platforms and verify whether they are deployed on the same hardware or on different hardware.
Verify the number of application servers in the system. If only one is installed, install another APP Server to enhance system performance. (Optional)
Ensure system is Unicode (if Non Unicode you need to convert Non-Unicode to Unicode.)
Data collection Job Should be Finished.

 

Step11 .

Download the Simplification Database.
Open the SAP Software Download Center.
Choose Types of Software Installations & Upgrades Access downloads and search for CCMSIDB.
Choose the relevant ZIP file and choose Download Basket.
Import the Simplification Database into the System.

 

Step12 .

Readiness & Simplification Item Check Prerequisite.
ST-PI and ST/API components need to upgrade as per requirement to the latest version.
SPAM/SAINT patch 71 (or higher) is required. - Version Upgrade.
Implement Note Analyzer (Optional) .
Implement the current version of SAP note 1668882 to update the Note Assistant.
Implement SAP Note 3200109 or upgrade to the corresponding Support Package for Note Analyzer.
Implement 2758146 - SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA & SAP Signavio Process Insights, Discovery Edition.
Implement 2913617 - SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA.
Implement 2399707 - Simplification Item Check.
Implement 2502552 - S4TC - SAP S/4HANA Conversion & Upgrade new Simplification Item Checks.

 

Step13 .

Execute Readiness Check.
Before executing Readiness Check report RTCCTOOL should be in Green Status. Otherwise some Jobs will cancel.
Update Simplification Item catalog.
go to se38 and run RC_COLLECT_ANALYSIS_DATA.
Check the Target S/4 HANA Version and select mandatory check box and schedule job.
There is some FDQ and TMW related job running in system. These all jobs is IMP and should be finished.
SE38 -> and /SDF/RC_START_CHECK
All Jobs should be finished properly.

 

Step14 .

Launch SAP Readiness Check application.
Run this URL ---> https://me.sap.com/readinesscheck/home
Click on the Start new Analysis.
Upload zip files to readiness check application.
Enter the name of the Analysis and upload the Zip file obtained from the Execution.
Open the analysis once it is ready (take couple of minutes).
Custom Code section is empty. You need to upload Custom Code zip file to get result.
Upload the zip file.
Click on the refresh and you will see Readiness Check report on Portal.

 

Step15 .

Run Simplification Item checks in 000 Client.
Login to 000 client.
SE38 -> /SDF/RC_START_CHECK.
Update the catalog if required.
Click on "New Relevance & Consistency Check as background job.
Click on execute.
Monitor the job from sm37.
Select the target version of S/4 HANA.
Click on "Display Last check Result"
Click on execute.
Anything under "Generic" & "Red" take action accordingly.
Rerun the consistency check once all cleanups are complete.

 

Step16 .

ABAPER Steps.

Custom Code Correction (ATC)-Setup
Central Check System should be on or above Basis release 7.52
Need to check how many SPDDs are in the current system.

 

Step17 .

SD/MM Steps.

Customer Vendor Integration (CVI) Steps
Mandatory Conversion of Customers/Vendors to Business Partners.
For the CVI implementation, it is required to identify and implement all CVI‑related SAP Notes.
CVI Must implemented in System before doing SI-Check. Otherwise report will not come proper.

 

Step18 .

Security Steps.

Landscape & Access Review.
Identify all systems (DEV, QAS, PRD, Sandbox).
Validate security access to: SU01,PFCG,SU24,SU25 and SAP_ALL.
Ensure Basis & Security admin roles are updated.

 

Step19 .

User Cleanup.
Lock/delete inactive users (last login > 90 days).
Remove SAP_ALL / SAP_NEW from dialog users.
Validate service, batch, RFC users.

 

Step20 .

Role Cleanup.
Remove obsolete roles.
Identify unused roles (via SUIM / AGR_USAGE).
Remove composite role redundancies.
The Security Team’s role is important, as they are responsible for verifying which transaction codes and authorizations are assigned to source system users.
Analyze Current Roles: Evaluate existing roles (single, composite, derived) using SUIM to identify active vs. dormant roles.
It is required to review and validate the transaction codes and authorizations in the source system.

 

Step21 .

FI Steps.

Finance Preparations: Run inconsistency Report , SI Analysis , Delta configuration
Prepare system for S/4HANA-specific settings (e.g., New GL, Asset Accounting).

 

Step22 .

Maintenance Planner (Basis Steps)

Generate the Latest xml file from SPAM and Upload in Maintenance Planner.
The system must be correctly maintained in the SAP Product License. Otherwise, errors may occur while creating the stack file in Maintenance Planner.
Check the third-Party add-on's and business functions and generate the Stack XML. Carefully create Stack file because your Conversation depends on this Stack file.

 

Step23 .

Operating System Step / File system requirement (Infra Steps.)

Validate Operating System Compatibility.
Update the SAP Host Agent to the latest version.
Migration Key Generate 317096 - Migration Key Generation for ABAP Systems.
Provide minimum 25GB under DIR_EXE.
Make sure scp command should be work properly to Transfer the file from Source to Target system. ( DMO with system move).
Create /media file system with enough Space.
Save the sidadm environment variables before starting the S/4 conversion.
Provide minimum 100GB of free space in /usr/sap/trans.
Add Hostfile entry and Server should be ping with IP and Hostname and Ping between from Source and Target Server.
Nslookup should be work properly
DNS Should be Configure Properly in Servers   (Network Step.)

 

Step24 .

Database Specific Requirements (Basis Steps.)
Run DB statistics for all the tables and Dictionary.
"Database User Password validity check (SYSTEM and SAPSR3 and Schema).
Make sure that your database password will not expire during the migration process."
Make sure that there is no schema/user mismatch of your SAP ABAP system. Please proceed as described in note 1086375 to repair this mismatch. If your DB is SQL.

 

Step25 .

Migration Server Step. (LT1)
22, 1128 , 1129 and 4237 Port need to opened and Telnet Should be completed Successfully.  (Network Team.).
On Migration Server we need to install SAP AS ABAP Stub Installation.
Telnet should be working from LT1 Server to Source system.

 

Step26 . Target Host S/4HANA App and Database Server Steps.
Target Servers Should be ready. S/4 Hana Vanilla Installation and Database Installation on Target.
Target Tenant DB System User Password must be available.
Target DB SYSTEMDB SYSTEM User Password must be available.
Target DB Schema Password must be available.
Target DB DBACOCKPIT User Password must be available.
Import DU HANAUMML128-70001054.ZIP on the target DB as per the on‑screen instructions. SAP Notes 2928582.
Target DB log_mode Parameter value should be overwrite in System and Tenant DB.

 

Step27 .

SUM Specific Requirements on Source and Target Server.
1128 and 1129 Port need to opened and Telnet Should be completed Successfully.
Create sapmedia File System with minimum 200 GB Space.
Keep all the Software in sapmedia File System.
Provide 777 Permission of sapmedia File System.
Take Full DB Backup and VM Snapshot of System before start the SUM. (VM Snapshot Backup is Optional)
Uncar the SUM.
Register the SUM.
Before starting SUM, run the SI Check report once again. The result must be Green.
If any errors appear in the SI Check report, do not launch SUM—first analyze and fix the errors.
Only proceed with SUM after the SI Check report is completely clean and Green."
Launch the SUM with SIDADM User.
Provide the Stack file in SUM.
Provide Migration Key.
DDIC of 000 and Password must be available.
You can choose here which Scenario you want to go DMO with System move.
Kindly follow and monitor the standard SUM screen for ongoing status and updates.

 

Conclusion:

SAP ECC to S/4HANA Conversion Checklist.

The SAP ECC to S/4HANA conversion is a very difficult that requires strong planning and system readiness.

This checklist highlights all the steps that need to be completed before starting the S/4HANA conversion.

 

Thanks.

entry.title: Technical Inquiry on Account Differentiation via BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD
entry.content:
In our S/4HANA Public Cloud environment, we are managing insurance policies (Prepaid Expenses - Account 180) using the Purchase Order Accruals process. We need to ensure that the system triggers different G/L accounts based on the "Policy Type" (Product Type Group) associated with the Purchase Order.

We need technical clarification on the following:

  1. BAdI Usage: Is BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD (Accrual Engine - Document Schema Cloud) the correct and recommended enhancement point to override standard account determination for PO-based accruals?

  2. Field Availability: Does this BAdI allow us to access the Product Type Group or other Purchase Order item-level fields to drive the conditional logic for G/L account selection?
  3. Scope: Can we use this BAdI to dynamically differentiate the Debit/Credit accounts for the accrual objects created from Purchase Orders?

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and ECTR
entry.content:
What Should R&D‑Heavy Customers Do?

SAP Engineering Control Center (ECTR) is positioned as a strategic solution for integrating mechanical and electrical CAD systems with SAP PLM and SAP S/4HANA. Many manufacturing customers rely on ECTR as a core component of their engineering and R&D processes

At the same time, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is increasingly positioned as the strategic ERP target— with clear benefits around SaaS, clean core, and innovation speed.

However, SAP ECTR is currently available only as an on‑premise solution and is not supported with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.

The Dilemma?

This creates a real challenge for customers who are:

  • R&D‑heavy and CAD‑centric
  • Already invested in SAP ECTR
  • Strategically interested in moving to S/4HANA Cloud, public edition

Today, these customers seem forced to choose between:

  • Keeping ECTR and staying on‑premise / private cloud, or
  • Moving to public cloud and rethinking core engineering processes

Neither option is trivial.

Typical Paths Discussed

  • Stay on‑premise or private cloud to keep full ECTR capabilities
  • Run a hybrid landscape, combining public cloud ERP with on‑premise ECTR
  • Adopt public‑cloud PLM services, accepting functional differences compared to ECTR

Each option comes with trade‑offs in complexity, functionality, and long‑term strategy.

Open Questions

I’d like to invite discussion from SAP and the community:

  1. How does SAP envision R&D‑heavy customers adopting S/4HANA Cloud, public edition without ECTR?
  2. Are hybrid scenarios a long‑term target architecture or only a transition phase?
  3. Is there a public‑cloud roadmap that could eventually cover ECTR‑like capabilities?

Closing

SAP ECTR is strategic.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition is strategic.

For many engineering‑driven customers, how these two strategies fit together is still unclear. I hope this post can help trigger an open and constructive discussion.

entry.title: Powering AI-Ready Enterprises with SAP Integration Suite - SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026
entry.content:
Hi Community,


Looking forward to SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026, where the spotlight is firmly on the future of integration, AI, and real-time enterprise architecture. This year’s sessions highlight how integration is rapidly evolving—from a backend capability into a strategic enabler for AI-driven business outcomes.

From consolidating integration landscapes to securely connecting and governing AI agents, and learning from real-world success stories like AstraZeneca, the agenda in Madrid truly showcases what’s next.

Don’t miss the opportunity to gain valuable insights, explore innovative approaches, and see how integration is becoming the backbone of AI-powered enterprises. 


Discover practical workflows for mapping, test creation, incident triage, and documentation, along with necessary controls for AI tool interacting with enterprise APIs and event streams. Attendees will receive a shortlist of safe pilots and guardrails to maintain auditability.

BTP1493: Enterprise-ready AI integration: Securely connect and govern your AI agents


Agentic AI requires a trusted integration foundation. A phased consolidation approach highlights where to standardize first, what to retain, and how to unify governance across APIs and events using API Management and advanced Event Mesh capabilities. Explore a practical scoring model that can help you prioritize migrations and advance AI readiness.

BTP1334: Why organizations consolidate integration tools to prepare for Al​


AstraZeneca’s Axial greenfield SAP transformation is building a single foundation on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) for integration, user experience, and automation. Hear what it uses across SAP BTP and learn about its API‑led and event‑driven integration patterns using Cloud Integration, advanced event mesh, and Edge Integration Cell.

BTP1494: Modernising enterprise integration with SAP BTP at AstraZeneca​


Don’t miss these sessions at SAP Sapphire Madrid—add them to your agenda and take the next step toward becoming an AI-ready enterprise.

entry.title: Re: MD4C Report
entry.content:
Try with the below approach

In MD4C, use the layout management (Settings → Layout → Change Layout). Add columns such as Delay in Days, Rescheduling Indicator, or Exception Messages. These replace the old icon.

Delays are now flagged via exception messages (e.g., “Reschedule in” or “Delay in procurement”). These appear in the exception column rather than as an icon.

entry.title: Re: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patc...

Unfortunately, I don't manage it, and whoever manages it assured me that putting the folders and the executables involved in the SAP product upgrade on the exclusion list, would be sufficient.

Thank you all guys.

entry.title: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patch 8 to SP5 patch 5 failed !
entry.content:
Hello guys
I'm having problems trying to upgrade an SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 system, from SP4 patch 8 to SP05 patch 5.
First of all, I report the data of the system:
- SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 SP04 Patch 08 single node.
- The web application server is Apache Tomcat 9.0.91.
- The CMS database is a locally installed SQL Anywhere 17 (17.11.7458).
- Everything is hosted by a Windows Server 2022 version 21H2 VM (OS Build 20348.4773).
- As CPU, the VM has 8 sockets and 16 virtual processors.
- The RAM amounts to 128GB.
The upgrade is necessary to close some security flaws found in the current version of the product (SAP notes 3654236, 3695912, 3697256).

The upgrade ended with errors.
During the process, I actually noticed that the process attempted to log in to the CMS twice, lasting a full 14 minutes.
After 14 minutes, it loggied an error in the "setupengine.log" file, and continued.
After the upgrade was complete, I found the following logs in the "ErrorsAndWarnings.log" file:

11:38:52.686 ERROR: Could not reach CMS '*****:6400'. Specify the correct host and port and check for network issues. Please refer to SAP Knowledge Base Article 2151044 on the SAP Support Portal. (FWM 20030)
*#*_*FWM 20030|ERROR: Could not reach CMS '*****:6400'. Specify the correct host and port and check for network issues, please refer to SAP Knowledge Base Article 2151044 on the SAP Support Portal. (FWM 20030)|ERROR: Could not reach CMS '*****:6400'. Specify the correct host and port and check for network issues, please refer to SAP Knowledge Base Article 2151044 on the SAP Support Portal. (FWM 20030)*_*#*
11:54:21.683 ERROR: Could not reach CMS '*****:6400'. Specify the correct host and port and check for network issues, please refer to SAP Knowledge Base Article 2151044 on the SAP Support Portal. (FWM 20030)

The strange thing is that, anyway, the system is now fully up and running. I can log in to both the CMC and BI Launchpad without any problems, there are no processing servers faulty, and even refreshing a test report is successful.

What happened?
Can I consider it successful?
Has this ever happened to you?

Thank you very much.

Andrea

entry.title: Missing standard Business Event for Credit Management (Credit Limit for BPs) in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hello Community,

We are currently designing a solution to replicate Credit changes in the Business Partners.

The Requirement: We need to trigger an asynchronous event whenever a Business Partner's credit data is updated in SAP Credit Management (FSCM) — for instance, when a credit limit is changed, or the exposure/risk category is updated the external sytem must be notified in near real-time to block or release sales orders accordingly.

While looking into the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, we noticed that there is no standard event for Credit Management. The standard  BusinessPartner.Changed event does not reliably or specifically cover changes happening exclusively within the FSCM credit segments. We were looking for something along the lines of CreditDataChanged or CreditLimit.Changed, but it does not seem to exist.

My Questions:

  1. Is there any standard Event Enablement topic natively available for SAP Credit Management that we might have missed?

  2. Is utilizing a BAdI to publish custom events to the Event Mesh still the best practice/officially recommended approach for this specific scenario?

Any insights or architectural recommendations would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order with same WBS or same linked Sales order
entry.content:

We have a requirement of implementing an automated invoicing routine that automatically identifies and uses the pair of Sales Order and Outbound to create the billing document. The routine will also update the Billing Document's output type EDI = Immediately and save it once again. 

This routine cannot take the customer PO as reference as in the past it created issues as it would combine all outbounds that were linked to the same customer PO and create a single invoice (lump sum).

This would cause rejection from our customer, as a single billing document per SO / Outbound is expected per delivery.

We need to introduce a batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order with the same WBS element or same linked sales order for sales Org. 
 
We checked for "schedule billing creation" app but parameter on the app is not satisfied. 

**We are working in a public cloud system**
**There was a post raised by my colleague but in that solution the mentioned BAdI(SD_BILLING_DOCUMENT_CREATE) couldn't be found in the system**
Link: Batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order ... - SAP Community

Thanks and Regards,
Arnab

entry.title: Re: Blog 4 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Par
entry.content:
I hope thats not the final Solution from SAP for Customer for AI Migration?
I was really expecting more than just an ABAP-to-SQL transformer powered by Claude — that's something you can already do today. You don't need SAP AI for that.

What I was hoping for is a specially trained AI agent provided by SAP that can identify all dependencies within a data flow, has a memory of the already migrated Objects (ADSO Z123 = SQL View ABC), remove outdated logic that's no longer state of the art (such as myself loops for updating master data within transaction data), and that has a built-in mapping from BW extractors to CDS views (so you can use DataProducts), implementes all the "possible" Logic in the Objectstore (so it's accessable for SAP Databricks and co. ).

In other words, an agent that intelligently migrates the entire data flow end to end.

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
I apologize for the faulty info with the previous answer regarding the 4 install options. I was looking at notes from an older installation version. I've updated the answer to include details from the latest installer. In particular, both the Full and Custom options do include the HADR product (under Custom it's called 'SAP ASE Data Movement for HADR').

HADR is a more involved product/solution. HADR requires a replication server, at least 2 ASE instances, and additional components for monitoring the entire setup.  HADR is not required for the basic ASE instance to function.

Since your primary objective appears to be the creation of a basic ASE instance I suggest that after selecting Custom you make sure the 'SAP ASE Data Movement for HADR' option is unchecked/not-selected.

As for the comment about not having problems with HADR when running 16.0,  I'm guessing either a) you didn't install HADR when you previously installed ASE 16.0 or b) you installed an older version of 16.0 that did not include HADR.  [I think HADR became available with 16.0 SP02, though don't quote me on that. This would mean that 16.0 and 16.0 SP01 installers did not have the HADR option.]

entry.title: SAP BDC - Driving Lite Viewer Adoption: Why Your Tables Matter
entry.content:
The Lite Weight Viewer (LWV) is one of the most impactful Client Side performance improvements SAP Analytics Cloud has shipped in the last few years. No configuration is needed for the viewer, it just kicks in automatically when a story is compatible.

That "when compatible" is where most stories are losing out.

Where we are today

Looking at our telemetry across the SAC landscape, one of the top three reasons stories fall back to the full viewer is Tables that haven't been migrated to the New Table Experience. Stories with classic table widgets get redirected out of the Lite Viewer silently. No error, no warning, just slower than it could be. 

For a refresher on what the Lite Viewer is and what conditions a story needs to meet, see the initial release blog post of the Initial Release of the Lite Viewer for SAP Analytics Cloud Stories 

How to check where you stand

The Feature Adoption section in the SAP Analytics Cloud System Overview shows you, per folder, the share of story opens going through Optimized and Lite Viewer. If a folder shows a low Lite Viewer share despite being in Optimized Experience, classic tables are worth checking.

Start with your high-traffic folders. You don't need to migrate everything,  focus where it has the most impact.

Prioritizing what to migrate 

A large tenant can have hundreds of Classic stories — most of them unused. Before touching tables, it's worth separating what's actively used from what can be cleaned up. The System Overview Housekeeping function and the Feature Adoption dashboard together give you that picture. For a practical walkthrough of that approach: Efficient Migration of SAC Classic Stories: Focusing on the Essentials

Migrating to the New Table Experience

With the 2025 Q3 QRC, SAP shipped a redesigned table building experience that is also what unlocks Lite Viewer compatibility. The migration itself is done directly in story edit mode. One thing to keep in mind: blended tables from the Classic Experience don't auto-migrate and need to be rebuilt manually.

Full details on what's new and how migration works: The New Table Building Experience in SAP Analytics Cloud 

In short

  1. Check the Feature Adoption section in System Overview — look for folders with a low Lite Viewer share
  2. Prioritize: focus on actively used stories, clean up the rest
  3. For those stories: verify they're in Optimized Experience, then check for classic tables
  4. Migrate the tables to the New Table Experience
  5. The Lite Viewer picks them up automatically from that point on

entry.title: Re: Check Stock Overview - Report Clarifications
entry.content:
The “Check Stock Overview” functionality can be accessed through different apps in Public Cloud (for example, Stock – Multiple Materials [F0843A], Stock Overview [F1595], or Stock – Single Material [F3366]). Each of these apps has slightly different logic for how inventory balances are displayed and reconciled with the G/L.

A few points to clarify for your case:

1) How balances are fetched
The report reads from the material ledger / inventory valuation tables and then cross‑checks against the G/L accounts defined in the valuation area.

2) GL Selection logic
The “GL Selection” option filters which inventory accounts are included. Only accounts linked to valuation classes and account determination in OBYC (Public Cloud equivalent customizing) will be picked up.

3) Why some GLs match and others don’t
Some inventory accounts may not be linked to valuation classes correctly. Certain postings (like manual journal entries or adjustments) bypass the material ledger and therefore don’t show in the stock overview. Timing differences can occur if you compare the report before material ledger closing.

Since you have not mentioned which app you are using, please check whether you are running F0843A, F1595, or F3366. The reconciliation behavior differs slightly:

  • F0843A (Stock – Multiple Materials): Best for comparing stock quantities and values across materials.
  • F1595 (Stock Overview): More focused on valuation and reconciliation with G/L.
  • F3366 (Stock – Single Material): Detailed drilldown for one material, including valuation class and G/L link.

If your issue is reconciliation with the Trial Balance, F1595 is usually the right app to use, because it is designed to align inventory values with the G/L. If you are using F0843A or F3366, the balances shown are more operational and may not reconcile directly with FI.

Having said that, If you are using F1595, then the mismatches usually point to postings outside of material ledger or incorrect account determination. If you are using F0843A/F3366, please note that those apps are not intended for direct reconciliation with the Trial Balance.

entry.title: The Trust Triad: A Playbook for Architecting Secure and Sovereign SAP Landscapes
entry.content:
Speaker: Dr. Michael Blaschke, Principal, Global SAP Enterprise Architecture Advisory

 Architecture, Security, and Sovereignty are no longer three separate conversations.  Join our webcast live to learn and discuss how to bring them together Silos between Architecture, Security, and Sovereignty are creating costly risks for SAP landscapes. In this webcast, Dr. Michael Blaschke introduces the “Trust Triad” — a practical playbook that brings these disciplines together early in design so security and sovereignty are designed in from the start, not retrofitted later. What you’ll learn:

  • The Trust Triad: converging Architecture, Security, and Sovereignty
  • SAP Shared Responsibility Model and how it affects design
  • Practical patterns to move security and sovereignty left into architecture
  • Adapting the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework for sovereign environments
  • Real-world examples from military and public-sector engagements

When architecture, security, and sovereignty are designed together, trust stops being a slogan and becomes an engineering discipline. Who should watch: enterprise architects, security officers, cloud strategy owners, and SAP program leads looking for concrete steps to make their SAP landscape secure, sovereign, and future-proof.

Bring your questions – we will dedicate the final 10 minutes to live Q&A. 

Join live or watch the replay in YouTube:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OZwsUXRs76Q

entry.title: Re: Screen and Transaction Variant MIRO Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear @talke,

The on-premise approach via SHD0 and OLMRLIST is not available in Public Cloud those tools are not exposed to the customer and customer-specific MIRO transaction or screen variants in the on-premise sense cannot be created. MIRO is delivered as a SAP GUI for HTML / Harmonized WebGUI transaction inside the Fiori launchpad. For supported classic applications, SAP provides the supported Public Cloud equivalent of SHD0-style screen tailoring Adapt UI for classic applications powered by SAP Screen Personas, embedded in Publiv Cloud. It is launched from the user action menu via Adapt UI and produces flavors role or user-specific variants of the original screen that do not modify the underlying transaction. Within a flavor a key user can hide and rearrange fields, rename labels set default values and automate steps with the embedded scripting engine, which covers most requirements typically addressed by SHD0

Two limitations to set expectations correctly:

  • flavors do not generate a separate transaction code and
  • there is no exposed equivalent of the OLMRLIST configuration table for MIRO field control

Where the process scope allows it, Create Supplier Invoice (F0859) is the basic Fiori alternative and can be adapted with standard SAPUI5 key-user extensibility (Adapt UI, App Variants, Custom Fields and Logic). F0859 is a simplified app that does not cover the full MIRO function scope, so it is only an option if the required process fits

If a specific layout or behaviour is achievable on-premise via SHD0/OLMRLIST but cannot be reproduced via flavors or F0859 extensibility the gap should be raised via SAP Customer Influence rather than worked around outside the cloud extensibility model

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/f9467657a11a0522e10000000a44147b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/3ace5ee99c924eaca6f31780980d0d31.html

https://learning.sap.com/courses/adapting-the-ui-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-using-sap-screen-personas

https://influence.sap.com/

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAPcloud版本 付款申请功能操作问题
entry.content:
Hi @LeoPengjl , 

Please find below the answers: 

Question 1 When maintaining a Purchase Order with down payment category, payment percentage, and due date at the header level, is there a way (enhancement or other method) to automatically populate these values into the PO line items?

Standard system behavior:

If the payment is added at the header level, header data cannot be distributed to line item data. This is due to the fact that the down payment on the header level is not split onto the item level, it is retained at the header level as a payment on the whole PO. When you create the down payment request for the down payment maintained at the header level, the system doesn't propose the down payment amount. You have to enter the down payment amount manually on the line item or items accordingly.

For details see the SAP Note 2894701 - Down payment amount in PO header not proposed when creating Down Payment Request - SAP for Me.

The recommended approach in S/4HC Public is:

  • Maintain down payment parameters directly at line item level, in the Create Purchase Order - Advanced app, go to the Invoice section of each item and maintain DP Category, Percentage, and Due Date there directly. This is SAP's intended design.

  • For automation between header and items: In Public Cloud, you can implement a Custom Logic (BAdI) via the Custom Logic app (ABAP Cloud / Key User Extensibility). You would need to find a released BAdI for PO save events, look for BAdIs within the MM_PUR_S4_PO area in the Extensibility Explorer app. However, note that down payment data can be recorded at both header and item level and is copied to Financial Accounting as default values, so the extensibility approach would need to programmatically copy header values down at save time.
  • If no suitable released BAdI exists, the only remaining option is a Side-by-Side extension on SAP BTP using the PurchaseOrder OData API to read the header and patch item values (but this adds architectural complexity).

Practical recommendation: Item-level maintenance is the correct and supported approach. If there are many line items, consider whether a Purchase Order template or contract-based approach that could reduce repetitive entry.

More information about  down payment you can find here:  https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/creating-purchase-orders-with-down-payments_c2ead69a-8fbd-4c62-9432-a8b8a2409b52

 

Question 2 After submitting a payment request in the "Create Payment Request" app, when the cashier goes to post the payment in the "Manage Payment Requests" app, is it possible to select multiple approved payment requests at once for posting?

Standard system behavior:

The Manage Payment Requests Fiori app in S/4HC Public Edition is designed for the approval and posting workflow of individual payment requests. In its current standard form, the cashier posting step does not natively support batch multi-select posting of multiple approved requests in one action, each request is typically processed individually.

The recommended approach in S/4HC Public is:

  • The Automatic Payment Transactions for Payment Requests app (F111) is the standard tool for processing multiple payment requests in one run. F111 in particular is designed for payment requests and can process multiple approved items in a single execution.

Fiori App Link: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F111')/S36

 

Question 3 When Finance creates a payment strategy, is it possible to consolidate the bank account fields (house bank, account ID, supplier bank type) into the same screen as the payment posting, instead of requiring the user to navigate to a separate screen, simplifying the user experience?

Standard system behavior:

In S/4HC Public Edition, maintaining the payment strategy with bank-related fields (house bank, account ID, supplier bank type) requires navigating across multiple sections or screens. This is standard Fiori app behavior and the layout is not freely customizable in the same way as on-premise.

Options available in Public Cloud

  • UI Adaptation (Key User): The UI Adaptation tool available in the Fiori Launchpad allows Key Users to rearrange, show/hide, and relabel fields within a single page's sections. If the bank fields are technically on the same underlying Fiori page but in a different section, a Key User may be able to bring them into better visual proximity. However, merging two separate navigation targets (pages) into one is not possible via UI Adaptation alone.
  • Custom Fiori App on BTP: If the two screens are genuinely separate Fiori applications (separate app IDs), the only way to achieve the "single screen" experience is to build a Custom Fiori App on SAP BTP that consumes the relevant OData APIs for both payment strategy and bank master data, presenting them in a unified UI. This is technically feasible but requires BTP development effort.

Recommendation: Start with UI Adaptation to check if a Key User can reduce the navigation friction within what's technically possible. If the bank fields are on a completely separate app, set the expectation with the client that this is a known Public Cloud constraint and log the improvement request with SAP. A BTP custom app is the only real technical workaround, and the cost/benefit should be evaluated.

 

Thank you, 

Elena P.

entry.title: Re: Initial Stock Wrong Price Change -> "Allow Open Statu...
entry.content:
#GenAI Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the reply:

Proposed Solution for Handling Incorrect Initial Stock Pricing

Given the situation where the initial stock was updated with incorrect prices and subsequently adjusted using MR21, your proposed solution seems reasonable. Here is a breakdown of the approach:

  1. Manual Posting: By manually clearing the Price Difference Account against the Initial Stock Charge Account, you ensure that the Inventory Account (BSX) aligns with the Initial Stock Charge Account. This step is crucial to correct the accounting records for month X-1.

  2. Skipping CKMLCP for X-1: Not executing CKMLCP for month X-1 and instead focusing on month X allows you to avoid carrying forward the incorrect price differences. This is a practical approach given the circumstances.

  3. Executing CKMLCP for Month X: By activating the ‘Allow Open Status for Previous Period’ flag 3329567 - Error ML4HMASTER226 occurs during selection step of Actual Costing Run in the Preparation step of CKMLCP, you can proceed with the Material Ledger Closing for month X without issues. This flag allows you to handle open periods effectively, ensuring that only the relevant price differences for month X are considered.

Considerations

  • Testing: As you have already tested this approach in a development environment and it worked without issues, it provides confidence in the solution’s viability.
  • Documentation: Ensure that all steps and decisions are well-documented for future reference and audit purposes.

Overall, this approach should help in rectifying the initial error and aligning the financial records correctly for the go-live period.

Kind regards,

Henrike

- Create custom entity
- Implement related ABAP Class
- Build Meta-Data Extension to custom entity
-  Expose Custom Entity in ServiceDefinition

- Enter Association [1..X] in Root-Interface View "as _ExtData..."
- Reference "_ExtData" as Association in Root-Interface View
- Reference "_ExtData" as Association in Root-Composition View

entry.title: Re: Mass creation of POs for Insurance Accruals and Polic...
entry.content:
Do not create multiple PO document types just to differentiate insurance categories. Document types are meant for broad process control (e.g., standard PO vs. subcontracting PO), not fine-grained categorization. Instead, SAP Best Practice is to use Product Type Groups (or Material Groups) to classify different insurance categories. You can duplicate or extend product type groups to represent categories like “Health Insurance,” “Property Insurance,” “Liability Insurance,” etc.

This approach ensures:

  • Granular reporting by category.
  • Clear integration with the Accrual Engine, since postings can be differentiated by product type group.
  • Simpler governance, avoiding proliferation of PO document types.

In Public Cloud, the recommended way to automate mass PO creation is via the standard API for Purchase Orders (API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV). This OData API supports bulk creation and integration scenarios, and it is the standard interface aligned with SAP Best Practices. You can use it to feed hundreds of insurance policy POs into the system programmatically, ensuring they are available for subsequent processing in the Accrual Engine. Alternative approaches (like mass upload templates or manual apps) are not scalable for hundreds of policies and the API route is the best practice.
